cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.
commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
judge_reasoning: The finding directly matches the CVE fix. The diff adds bounds checks for both header->insnum and header->patnum. The finding correctly identifies that an unchecked patnum from a file leads to heap-based buffer overflow when accessing phead[i], which is exactly the CWE-787 heap overflow described in CVE-2019-14464.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The S3M loader reads header->insnum and header->patnum directly from the file without validating them against the pre-allocated array sizes (MP_MAXINS for instruments, 256 for patterns). A crafted file with excessively large values causes heap-based buffer overflows when accessing module->instr[], module->smp[], and module->phead[] arrays.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on phead[] array using file-controlled patnum
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The value header->patnum is read directly from the file and used as the loop bound when iterating over patterns. The code accesses phead[i] without checking whether i exceeds the pre-allocated size of the module->phead array. A crafted file with a large pattern count will cause out-of-bounds heap access when calling convertS3MPattern(&phead[i], ...), potentially leading to memory corruption and code execution. This is fixed in the benign version by adding a check that patnum does not exceed 256.

judge_findings.reasoning: The finding directly matches the CVE fix. The diff adds bounds checks for both header->insnum and header->patnum. The finding correctly identifies that an unchecked patnum from a file leads to heap-based buffer overflow when accessing phead[i], which is exactly the CWE-787 heap overflow described in CVE-2019-14464.
judge_findings.human_notes: Model correctly identified unchecked file-controlled patnum indexing into pre-allocated phead[] array. Finding matches CVE fix exactly. Model inferred OOB without knowing exact array size since allocation happens outside this function.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.
commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
judge_reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2008-3526 vulnerability: the bounds check on sca_keylength fails to subtract sizeof(struct sctp_authkey), allowing up to 8 bytes of over-read. This matches the exact one-line fix in the diff.

The `<=` check is unchanged in the fix and is not part of the vulnerability. It correctly rejects optlen values that are too small to contain the struct plus at least one byte of key data. This finding describes an unrelated edge case, not the CVE.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The bounds check `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen` should have been `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` to properly account for the struct header size. Without this, a crafted sca_keylength could cause up to 8 bytes (sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)) of buffer over-read, leading to denial of service or other impact.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Insufficient validation of sca_keylength allows out-of-bounds read of heap memory
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The check `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen` does not account for the size of the `struct sctp_authkey` header itself. The key data begins at an offset of `sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` within the allocated buffer, so the actual available space for key bytes is `optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`. An attacker can set `sca_keylength` to a value greater than the actual key data provided but still less than or equal to `optlen`, causing `sctp_auth_set_key` to read beyond the user-supplied data into uninitialized heap memory. This can leak sensitive kernel heap data or cause other undefined behavior. The correct check should be `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Boundary check uses <= instead of < allowing zero-length key allocation edge case
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The check `optlen <= sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` uses less-than-or-equal, meaning when `optlen == sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` it is rejected but `optlen == sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1` is accepted. Combined with the insufficient sca_keylength validation described above, this exacerbates the ability to provide a minimal buffer while claiming a larger key length, enabling heap over-read in the downstream `sctp_auth_set_key` function.

judge_findings.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2008-3526 vulnerability: the bounds check on sca_keylength fails to subtract sizeof(struct sctp_authkey), allowing up to 8 bytes of over-read. This matches the exact one-line fix in the diff.

The `<=` check is unchanged in the fix and is not part of the vulnerability. It correctly rejects optlen values that are too small to contain the struct plus at least one byte of key data. This finding describes an unrelated edge case, not the CVE.

cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.
commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
judge_reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-38383: a use-after-free in net_bind() where ptr->ai_addr/ai_addrlen are accessed after freeaddrinfo() has freed the memory. The CWE-416 matches, and the description aligns with the diff which introduces a local naddr buffer to avoid accessing freed memory.
judge_actual_issue_summary: Use-after-free in net_bind(): after freeaddrinfo(servinfo) frees all addrinfo structures, the code still accesses ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen via getsockname() and net_port_get/net_address_get calls. The fix uses a local union net_sockaddr buffer instead.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Use-after-free: ptr->ai_addr is accessed after freeaddrinfo() has freed the memory
vuln_only_findings.long_description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, all addrinfo nodes (including the one pointed to by `ptr`) are freed. However, `ptr` is subsequently dereferenced to access `ptr->ai_addr` and `ptr->ai_addrlen` in the getsockname() call and later in net_port_get/net_address_get. This is a use-after-free bug. Depending on the allocator behavior, this could lead to reading/writing corrupted or attacker-influenced heap data, potentially causing crashes or, in some scenarios, code execution. The fix is to copy the needed sockaddr into a local buffer before calling freeaddrinfo(), or to delay freeaddrinfo() until after the data is no longer needed.

judge_findings.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-38383: a use-after-free in net_bind() where ptr->ai_addr/ai_addrlen are accessed after freeaddrinfo() has freed the memory. The CWE-416 matches, and the description aligns with the diff which introduces a local naddr buffer to avoid accessing freed memory.

cve_desc: This affects the package drogonframework/drogon before 1.7.5. The unsafe handling of file names during upload using HttpFile::save() method may enable attackers to write files to arbitrary locations outside the designated target folder.
commit_message: Prevent malformed upload path causing arbitrary write (#1174)
judge_reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue: the unsanitized fileName_ (from user-supplied upload filename) is appended to the path, allowing path traversal sequences to write files outside the upload directory. This matches CVE-2022-25297 exactly. CWE-22 (Path Traversal) is closely related to the official CWE-552 (Files Accessible to External Parties) and describes the same underlying vulnerability mechanism.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The HttpFile::save() method used the user-controlled fileName_ member directly when constructing the final save path (fsPath / fsFileName), without sanitizing path traversal sequences. This allowed attackers to write uploaded files to arbitrary locations outside the upload directory. The fix refactors the API so the caller provides the full filename, removing the implicit use of the unsanitized fileName_ member.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Path traversal via unsanitized fileName_ allows writing files to arbitrary locations
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The member variable fileName_ (typically derived from a user-supplied multipart upload filename) is used directly in constructing the final file path without any sanitization. An attacker can supply a filename containing path traversal sequences such as "../../etc/cron.d/malicious" which would cause the file to be written outside the intended upload directory. This can lead to arbitrary file write, potentially enabling remote code execution (e.g., overwriting configuration files, cron jobs, or web-accessible scripts).

cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.
commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
judge_reasoning: This exactly describes the CVE. The fix adds a check `temp[bn_size - 1] != '\0'` to ensure the buffer is null-terminated before passing it to gimp_any_to_utf8 with length -1. CWE-126 (Buffer Over-read) is closely related to CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read).
This describes a file descriptor leak, which is unrelated to CVE-2017-17784. The CVE is about a heap-based buffer over-read due to missing null-terminator validation on brush name data.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The brush name data read from the file was passed to gimp_any_to_utf8 with length -1 (meaning treat as null-terminated string) without verifying that the buffer actually contained a null terminator. A crafted .gbr file could omit the null terminator, causing a heap-based buffer over-read. The fix adds a check that temp[bn_size - 1] == '\0'.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Brush name buffer passed as null-terminated string without guaranteed null terminator
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The `temp` buffer of size `bn_size` is read from the file and then passed to `gimp_any_to_utf8` with a length of -1, which means the function treats it as a null-terminated C string. However, the file data is not guaranteed to contain a null terminator within the `bn_size` bytes. This can cause the function to read beyond the allocated heap buffer until it finds a null byte, leading to an out-of-bounds heap read.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: File descriptor leak when header validation fails
vuln_only_findings.long_description: When the header sanitization check fails, the function returns -1 without calling close(fd), leaking the open file descriptor. This can lead to file descriptor exhaustion in long-running applications.

judge_findings.reasoning: This exactly describes the CVE. The fix adds a check `temp[bn_size - 1] != '\0'` to ensure the buffer is null-terminated before passing it to gimp_any_to_utf8 with length -1. CWE-126 (Buffer Over-read) is closely related to CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read).
This describes a file descriptor leak, which is unrelated to CVE-2017-17784. The CVE is about a heap-based buffer over-read due to missing null-terminator validation on brush name data.

cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.
commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
judge_reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact issue fixed by the commit: quantum_info is allocated via AcquireQuantumInfo but never destroyed with DestroyQuantumInfo before the ExitLoop label. The fix adds exactly `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info);` at that point. While the CVE description emphasizes the out-of-bounds read in CalcMinMax, this specific commit addresses the missing cleanup of quantum_info, and the finding accurately identifies that problem. The lack of proper cleanup could contribute to the vulnerability's exploitability (e.g., stale quantum_info state across loop iterations contributing to incorrect buffer access in CalcMinMax).
judge_actual_issue_summary: The fix adds `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info);` after the main scanline reading loop, before the ExitLoop label. This ensures quantum_info is properly freed between loop iterations, preventing a resource leak and potential use of stale quantum state that could contribute to the out-of-bounds read in CalcMinMax when processing crafted MAT files.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Memory leak of quantum_info on several exit paths.
vuln_only_findings.long_description: quantum_info is allocated via AcquireQuantumInfo but is never freed in the function. When execution reaches 'done_reading' or 'ExitLoop' labels, or even in the normal loop iteration where BImgBuff is freed but quantum_info is not, the QuantumInfo structure is leaked. While primarily a resource leak, repeated exploitation (e.g., processing many crafted images) could lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

The fix adds exactly `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info);` at that point. While the CVE description emphasizes the out-of-bounds read in CalcMinMax, this specific commit addresses the missing cleanup of quantum_info, and the finding accurately identifies that problem. The lack of proper cleanup could contribute to the vulnerability's exploitability (e.g., stale quantum_info state across loop iterations contributing to incorrect buffer access in CalcMinMax).

cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).
commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
judge_reasoning: This finding describes a hypothetical OOB read on empty skb, which is not the vulnerability addressed by CVE-2022-26878. The CVE is about a memory leak due to missing kfree_skb in the default case.

This exactly matches CVE-2022-26878: a memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle() where skbs with invalid packet types are never freed. The fix adds kfree_skb in the default case. CWE-401 (missing deallocation) aligns with CWE-772.
judge_actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle(): when a packet with an unrecognized type is received, the switch statement has no default case, so the sk_buff is never freed. The fix adds a default case that calls kfree_skb(skb).

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when skb has zero length data
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The function dereferences skb->data to read the packet type byte without first verifying that the skb contains at least 1 byte of data (i.e., skb->len >= 1). If a malicious or malformed virtio device provides an empty skb, this results in reading memory beyond the valid buffer. An attacker controlling the virtio backend could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing a crash.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Memory leak when packet type does not match any known type
vuln_only_findings.long_description: When pkt_type does not match any of the handled cases (HCI_EVENT_PKT, HCI_ACLDATA_PKT, HCI_SCODATA_PKT, HCI_ISODATA_PKT), the switch statement falls through without freeing the skb. This means the sk_buff is never freed, resulting in a kernel memory leak. A malicious virtio backend could repeatedly send packets with unrecognized type values to exhaust kernel memory, leading to a denial of service.

judge_findings.reasoning: This finding describes a hypothetical OOB read on empty skb, which is not the vulnerability addressed by CVE-2022-26878. The CVE is about a memory leak due to missing kfree_skb in the default case.

This exactly matches CVE-2022-26878: a memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle() where skbs with invalid packet types are never freed. The fix adds kfree_skb in the default case. CWE-401 (missing deallocation) aligns with CWE-772.

cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)
commit_message:
Initialize the max upload file size when quotas are enabled

Due to an unwanted check, files causing the quota to be exceeded
were deleted after the upload, but not during the upload.

The bug was introduced in 2009 in version 1.0.23

Spotted by @DroidTest, thanks!
judge_reasoning: The CVE is about an incorrect quota check that allows unlimited file uploads. The `max_filesize` is initialized to -1, and the condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` prevents the assignment of the actual quota limit, so files can be uploaded without size restriction. The finding describes a completely unrelated TOCTOU race condition issue.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The max_filesize variable is initialized to -1, and the quota check condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` prevents max_filesize from ever being set to the actual quota-based limit (user_quota_size - quota.size). This means uploads are never limited by the file size quota during transfer, allowing unbounded file uploads. The fix removes the `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` guard so that max_filesize is always computed from the quota.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: TOCTOU race between checknamesanity and file open/rename operations
vuln_only_findings.long_description: There is a time-of-check-to-time-of-use race condition between `checknamesanity(name, ...)` and the subsequent file operations (open, rename). An attacker could potentially manipulate the filesystem (e.g., replacing a directory component with a symlink) between the sanity check and the actual file operation. While O_NOFOLLOW mitigates the final symlink component, intermediate path components could still be exploited for path traversal, potentially allowing writes to unintended locations.

judge_findings.reasoning: The CVE is about an incorrect quota check that allows unlimited file uploads. The `max_filesize` is initialized to -1, and the condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` prevents the assignment of the actual quota limit, so files can be uploaded without size restriction. The finding describes a completely unrelated TOCTOU race condition issue.

cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.
commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
judge_reasoning: The CVE is about out-of-bounds read due to missing validation of offsets computed from ELF header fields (e.g., shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name) before using them to access the mmap'd file. The fix adds validated_offset() checks. This finding describes a hypothetical stack buffer overflow in the flag array, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: Out-of-bounds read when computing section name pointers (elf_map + shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name) from crafted ELF files without validating that the resulting pointer falls within the mmap'd file boundaries. The fix adds validated_offset() checks to ensure the name pointer is within bounds before use.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Potential stack buffer overflow in 4-byte 'flag' buffer
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The 'flag' buffer is only 4 bytes. It is initialized with "   " (3 chars + null = 4 bytes exactly). The functions flag2str_sh() and flag2str() write into this buffer, but if they write more than 3 characters (plus null terminator), a stack buffer overflow occurs. The buffer size provides very little margin for the flag string representation.

judge_findings.reasoning: The CVE is about out-of-bounds read due to missing validation of offsets computed from ELF header fields (e.g., shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name) before using them to access the mmap'd file. The fix adds validated_offset() checks. This finding describes a hypothetical stack buffer overflow in the flag array, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

judge_reasoning: The actual CVE is about missing validation that input and rhs tensors have the same batch (outer) dimensions. The fix adds a loop checking `input.dim_size(dim) == rhs.dim_size(dim)` for all outer dimensions. This finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow issue unrelated to the actual vulnerability.
The actual CVE is about missing validation of batch dimensions between input and rhs tensors. This finding describes a hypothetical integer truncation issue unrelated to the actual vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The MatrixSolve GPU op was missing validation that the outer (batch) dimensions of the input matrix and right-hand side tensor match. When inputs had different batch dimensions, this could lead to crashes or silent incorrect behavior. The fix adds a loop to verify that all outer dimensions are equal.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Integer overflow in scratch space size calculation and integer truncation in batch loop
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The expression `sizeof(Scalar*) * batch_size` can overflow since `batch_size` is `int64`. If `batch_size` is very large, the multiplication wraps around, causing a smaller-than-needed buffer to be allocated. Subsequently, the loop `for (int batch = 0; batch < batch_size; ++batch)` uses an `int` loop variable compared against an `int64` value. If `batch_size` exceeds INT_MAX, the `int` variable will overflow, leading to undefined behavior. Combined, these issues can lead to heap buffer overflow when writing pointer values into the undersized scratch buffer.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Implicit truncation of int64 dimension `n` when passed to cuBLAS/cuSOLVER APIs expecting int
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The variable `n` is declared as `int64` but cuBLAS and cuSOLVER APIs (GetrfBatched, Getrf, Getrs, GetrsBatched) typically accept `int` parameters. If `n` exceeds INT_MAX, the implicit conversion truncates the value, which could cause incorrect buffer size calculations inside the CUDA libraries, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access on the GPU. Similarly, `nrhs` and `batch_size` are `int64` values passed where `int` is expected.

cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.
commit_message: Fixed handling of empty JPEG-LS scans.
judge_reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-32978) is about an assertion failure when `lines` is 0 due to an empty JPEG-LS scan. The fix replaces `assert(lines > 0)` with a graceful `if (lines == 0) return false;`. The finding describes a completely different issue about DecodeRun potentially exceeding line buffer bounds, which is unrelated to the empty scan / assertion failure vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: When an empty JPEG-LS scan is encountered, `lines` can be 0 while the code has `assert(lines > 0)`, causing an assertion failure (crash in debug builds) or undefined behavior in the `do { } while(--lines)` loop (in release builds, decrementing 0 wraps around). The fix adds a proper check to return early when lines is 0.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when decoded run length exceeds remaining line length
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The value returned by DecodeRun() comes from the encoded bitstream and may exceed the remaining `length` of the line. The inner `while(run)` loop decrements both `run` and `length`, but only checks `run`. If `run > length`, the `lp[cx]` pointers advance past the end of the allocated line buffer, causing out-of-bounds writes. After the loop, `length` is negative, so the `if (length)` check is true (non-zero), causing additional OOB writes. Furthermore, the outer `do { } while(--length)` loop continues iterating with a large negative value (wrapping through a huge range), resulting in massive heap corruption. A crafted JPEG-LS file can exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

judge_findings.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-32978) is about an assertion failure when `lines` is 0 due to an empty JPEG-LS scan. The fix replaces `assert(lines > 0)` with a graceful `if (lines == 0) return false;`. The finding describes a completely different issue about DecodeRun potentially exceeding line buffer bounds, which is unrelated to the empty scan / assertion failure vulnerability.

cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.
commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)

          * zero in the case the queue is used as a semaphore. */

         /* Check for multiplication overflow. */
         configASSERT( ( uxItemSize == 0 ) || ( uxQueueLength == ( xQueueSizeInBytes / uxItemSize ) ) );
+
+        /* Check for addition overflow. */
+        configASSERT( ( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes ) >  xQueueSizeInBytes );

judge_reasoning: The CVE is about an out-of-range double-to-unsigned-long cast at the lines `width=(size_t) floor(bounds.x2-bounds.x1+0.5)` and `height=(size_t) floor(...)`, fixed by wrapping with CastDoubleToLong(). The finding describes a completely different issue — a hypothetical off-by-one buffer overflow in the command buffer parsing loop, which is not what CVE-2022-32546 addresses.
judge_actual_issue_summary: When crafted input provides extremely large values for bounds (e.g., 4e+26), casting the result of `floor(bounds.x2-bounds.x1+0.5)` directly to `size_t` (unsigned long) produces undefined behavior because the double value is outside the representable range. The fix wraps these casts with `CastDoubleToLong()` to safely handle out-of-range values.

vuln_only_findings.short_description: Off-by-one heap/stack buffer overflow when writing null terminator to the command buffer
vuln_only_findings.long_description: The character is written to the buffer via `*p++=(char) c` before the bounds check is evaluated. When p reaches `command + MagickPathExtent - 1`, the write at that index is valid, but p is then incremented to `command + MagickPathExtent`. If the character is not '/' or '\n', the size check `(p-command) < (MagickPathExtent-1)` fails (it equals MagickPathExtent), so execution falls through to `*p = '\0'`, which writes one byte past the end of the `command` buffer (at index MagickPathExtent). This is a classic off-by-one buffer overflow that can corrupt adjacent stack variables. An attacker could craft a PCL file with a long line (no '/' or '\n' characters) to trigger this overflow, potentially overwriting adjacent local variables like `density`, `filename`, `geometry`, `options`, or `input_filename` pointers/buffers, leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
commit_message:
Prevent null dereference read in `GetInitOp`.

We have a map of maps. We test that the key exists in the first map but then we don't have any validation that this also means the second map has the needed key. In the scenarios where this is not the case, we'll dereference a nullptr, if we don't have this check

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408739325
Change-Id: If9bb7ed759aba1f3b56a34913f209508dbaf65ce
judge_reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23577: the unchecked find() result on the outputs map leads to dereferencing an end() iterator (null pointer dereference), which is precisely what the fix addresses.
judge_actual_issue_summary: In GetInitOp, after finding kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in the signature_def map, the code calls find() on the outputs sub-map but never checks if the key exists there before dereferencing the iterator. If the outputs map lacks that key, dereferencing the end() iterator causes a null pointer dereference crash.

vuln_only_findings.description: When the signature_def map contains `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the code looks up the same key in the `outputs()` map of that signature definition. The result of `find()` is never checked against `end()` before being dereferenced. If the outputs map does not contain `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the iterator returned by `find()` equals `end()`, and dereferencing it is undefined behavior (typically a null/invalid pointer dereference or out-of-bounds read).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a `meta_graph_def` whose `signature_def()` map contains an entry keyed by `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`.

state.value: valid iterator pointing to the signature def entry

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `init_op_sig_it != sig_def_map.end()` passes, so execution enters the if-block.

`init_op_sig_it->second.outputs()` returns the outputs map of the found SignatureDef. `.find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey)` is called on this outputs map. If the outputs map does not contain `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the returned iterator equals `outputs().end()`.
The code immediately dereferences this past-the-end iterator via `->second.name()`, causing undefined behavior (read of invalid memory).

judge_findings.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23577: the unchecked find() result on the outputs map leads to dereferencing an end() iterator (null pointer dereference), which is precisely what the fix addresses.

The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
judge_reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2022-21730: missing validation that row/col pooling sequence values are non-negative, leading to out-of-bounds heap access. The fix adds exactly these checks (in_row_start >= 0 && in_row_end >= 0, in_col_start >= 0 && in_col_end >= 0).
judge_actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor values are non-negative. Negative values in these tensors would produce negative indices used to access heap-allocated buffers via coeffRef(), causing out-of-bounds heap reads. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring both row and column sequence tensor values are >= 0.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vuln_only_findings.description: The values read from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor (in_row_start, in_col_start) are never validated to be >= 0 or < in_rows/in_cols. While in_row_end/in_col_end are clamped with std::min against the max index, in_row_start and in_col_start are used unchecked. An attacker can supply negative values or values exceeding in_rows/in_cols, causing in_index to go out of bounds of the in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat buffer, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Values in_row_start and in_col_start are read from attacker-controlled row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor without lower-bound validation.
in_index is computed using unchecked in_r/in_c derived from in_row_start/in_col_start, resulting in an out-of-bounds index.
coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses memory out of bounds, causing OOB read/write.

judge_findings.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2022-21730: missing validation that row/col pooling sequence values are non-negative, leading to out-of-bounds heap access. The fix adds exactly these checks (in_row_start >= 0 && in_row_end >= 0, in_col_start >= 0 && in_col_end >= 0).

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.
commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
judge_reasoning: The finding describes a scenario where setuid()/setgid() fails and the internal state is incorrectly updated. However, CVE-2019-18276 is about a different issue: when setuid() SUCCEEDS, it still doesn't drop the saved UID on Linux systems. An attacker can then call setuid() again (via a loaded shared object) to regain the original elevated privileges because the saved UID was preserved. The fix replaces setuid()/setgid() with setresuid()/setresgid() to explicitly set the saved UID/GID as well. The finding identifies a tangential error-handling concern, not the actual CVE vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: CVE-2019-18276: disable_priv_mode() used setuid()/setgid() which on Linux does not clear the saved UID/GID. This allows an attacker to regain privileges by calling setuid(saved_uid) from a runtime-loaded shared object. The fix uses setresuid()/setresgid() to explicitly set real, effective, AND saved UIDs/GIDs all to the real UID/GID.

vuln_only_findings.description: If setuid() or setgid() fails, the function logs an error but continues execution (unless EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is defined and errno is EAGAIN). It then unconditionally updates current_user.euid and current_user.egid to reflect dropped privileges, even though the actual process credentials were NOT changed. The program will subsequently believe it is running unprivileged when it is still running with elevated privileges.

step_by_step_execution.description: setuid(current_user.uid) is called to drop privileges. Suppose it fails (returns < 0) with an errno other than EAGAIN (e.g., EPERM).
sys_error logs the failure. The #if defined(EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE) block either does not exist (not compiled in) or the errno is not EAGAIN, so exit() is not called. Execution falls through.
setgid(current_user.gid) is called. It may also fail; if so, another error is logged but execution continues.
current_user.euid is set to current_user.uid and current_user.egid is set to current_user.gid, even though the real process credentials have not been changed.

The program now believes privileges have been dropped. Any subsequent security check relying on current_user.euid will incorrectly conclude the process is unprivileged, allowing privileged operations to continue unchecked.

judge_findings.reasoning: The finding describes a scenario where setuid()/setgid() fails and the internal state is incorrectly updated. However, CVE-2019-18276 is about a different issue: when setuid() SUCCEEDS, it still doesn't drop the saved UID on Linux systems. An attacker can then call setuid() again (via a loaded shared object) to regain the original elevated privileges because the saved UID was preserved. The fix replaces setuid()/setgid() with setresuid()/setresgid() to explicitly set the saved UID/GID as well. The finding identifies a tangential error-handling concern, not the actual CVE vulnerability.

cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.
commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
judge_reasoning: This finding describes a potential use-of-uninitialized-pointer issue, not the divide-by-zero vulnerability (CVE-2014-0142). The CVE is specifically about s->tracks being zero and causing a division by zero in seek_to_sector. Additionally, in QEMU, bs->opaque is typically zero-initialized, so s->catalog_bitmap would be NULL, making g_free safe. This finding is unrelated to the actual CVE.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The parallels block driver reads s->tracks from the disk image header without checking if it is zero. This value is later used as a divisor in seek_to_sector(), causing a divide-by-zero crash (denial of service) when a malicious image has tracks=0. The fix adds a check rejecting images with zero tracks.

vuln_only_findings.description: If the first `bdrv_pread` call (reading the header) fails, or if the magic/version check fails, the code jumps to the `fail` label which calls `g_free(s->catalog_bitmap)`. However, `s->catalog_bitmap` has not been initialized at that point, so `g_free` is called on an uninitialized pointer, which can lead to freeing an arbitrary memory address.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins. `s->catalog_bitmap` is not initialized and contains whatever garbage value was in the `BDRVParallelsState` structure.
`bdrv_pread(bs->file, 0, &ph, sizeof(ph))` is called to read the header. Suppose this call fails and returns a negative value.
The check `if (ret < 0)` is true, so execution jumps to the `fail` label.
At the `fail` label, `g_free(s->catalog_bitmap)` is called with the uninitialized pointer value, which can attempt to free an arbitrary address, potentially causing a crash or memory corruption.

judge_findings.reasoning: This finding describes a potential use-of-uninitialized-pointer issue, not the divide-by-zero vulnerability (CVE-2014-0142). The CVE is specifically about s->tracks being zero and causing a division by zero in seek_to_sector. Additionally, in QEMU, bs->opaque is typically zero-initialized, so s->catalog_bitmap would be NULL, making g_free safe. This finding is unrelated to the actual CVE.

This finding describes a completely different issue - a potential heap buffer overflow in the files array management logic, which is unrelated to the CVE.
judge_actual_issue_summary: Use-after-free when searching for pattern in path. The function uses `ml_get()` which returns a pointer to internal buffer memory that can be freed/reallocated by subsequent operations (like `ins_compl_add_infercase`). The fix replaces three `ml_get()` calls with `get_line_and_copy()` which copies the line content into the `file_line` buffer, preventing use of freed memory.

vuln_only_findings.description: Heap buffer overflow when the reallocation of the `files` array fails (bigger == NULL) and no previously visited files have been closed yet (old_files == max_path_depth). In this case, `files[depth + 1]` accesses `files[old_files]` which equals `files[max_path_depth]`, one element past the end of the allocated array. Subsequent writes to `files[depth].name`, `.lnum`, and `.matched` are also out-of-bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The files array is allocated with max_path_depth elements (initially 50) and old_files is set to max_path_depth.

Through 50 iterations of the main loop, each line in the current buffer and each opened include file contains an include directive pointing to a new file. Each iteration increments depth by 1 (from -1 to 0, 1, 2, ..., 49). No files reach EOF so old_files remains 50.

On the 51st include, the condition `depth + 1 == old_files` is true (50 == 50). ALLOC_MULT is called to resize the array. Under memory pressure, this allocation fails and bigger is NULL. The code falls through without resizing.
The code accesses files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...), which is files[50].fp — one past the end of the 50-element array. This is a heap out-of-bounds write.

If mch_fopen succeeds, ++depth makes depth == 50 == old_files. The code then writes to files[old_files].name (OOB read/free), increments old_files, then writes files[depth].name, files[depth].lnum, files[depth].matched — all at index 50, which is out-of-bounds.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

cve_desc: e2guardian v5.4.x <= v5.4.3r is affected by missing SSL certificate validation in the SSL MITM engine. In standalone mode (i.e., acting as a proxy or a transparent proxy), with SSL MITM enabled, e2guardian, if built with OpenSSL v1.1.x, did not validate hostnames in certificates of the web servers that it connected to, and thus was itself vulnerable to MITM attacks.
commit_message:
Fix bug #707 cert hostnames not being checked
- only happened when openssl v1.1 is used
judge_reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-44273) is specifically about missing hostname validation in server certificates when using OpenSSL v1.1.x. The fix adds X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_host() to check that the server certificate's hostname matches. The finding instead claims SSL_CTX_set_verify(SSL_VERIFY_PEER) is missing, which is a different issue (peer certificate verification vs hostname verification). The actual fix does not add SSL_CTX_set_verify either, confirming this is not the vulnerability addressed by the CVE. The finding describes a different (possibly non-existent) problem.
judge_actual_issue_summary: When built with OpenSSL v1.1.x, e2guardian's SSL client connection code did not call X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_host() to validate that the server certificate's hostname matched the expected hostname. The fix adds this call for OpenSSL >= 1.1.0, preventing MITM attacks using certificates valid for different hostnames.

vuln_only_findings.description: The function never calls SSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, SSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...). By default, OpenSSL client connections use SSL_VERIFY_NONE, meaning the server's certificate is never validated against the loaded CA certificates. An attacker performing a man-in-the-middle attack can present any certificate and the connection will succeed, despite the code going through the effort of loading verify locations and setting X509 params.

cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.
commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
judge_reasoning: This is a downstream consequence of the same root cause as CVE-2009-0689. When k is too large (due to a large precision value in printf), the lack of bounds checking allows integer overflow in the shift and incorrect memory allocation, leading to heap buffer overflow. The fix adds k > Kmax check which prevents this path.

This directly matches CVE-2009-0689's description as an 'array index error' in dtoa. The fix explicitly adds 'if (k > Kmax)' bounds checking before accessing freelist[k], which is exactly the primary vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: CVE-2009-0689 is an array index error in dtoa's Balloc function where the parameter k (derived from a large precision value in printf format) is used without bounds checking as an index into the freelist[] array and in a bit shift operation. The fix adds a bounds check (k > Kmax) to prevent out-of-bounds array access, and also adds a NULL check after MALLOC to handle allocation failures.

undesired_operation.description: When k is sufficiently large (e.g., k >= 31 on systems with 32-bit int), the expression '1 << k' produces undefined behavior (signed integer overflow) or wraps to a small/negative value. This causes the MALLOC to allocate a buffer much smaller than expected, while rv->maxwds is set to the corrupted value of x. Subsequent code that writes up to maxwds words into the Bigint will overflow the undersized heap buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — an undersized buffer is allocated but maxwds records a large or wrapped value, allowing subsequent writes to overflow the heap, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

step_by_step_execution.description: x = 1 << k is computed. With k=31 and 32-bit signed int, this is undefined behavior. On typical two's complement implementations it produces INT_MIN (0x80000000 = -2147483648).

MALLOC is called with sizeof(Bigint) + (x-1)*sizeof(Long). With x = -2147483648, (x-1) = -2147483649 which wraps in unsigned arithmetic. On a 32-bit size_t, this can result in a small allocation. On 64-bit, the cast to size_t of the negative multiplication result also produces a huge or wrapped value. In either case the allocation size is incorrect relative to maxwds.

cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate
commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
judge_reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2020-36406 vulnerability: the drain() check at function entry is insufficient because multiple triggeredTopics increments can occur within the loop and via recursion, causing a stack-based buffer overflow of the fixed-size 64-element triggeredTopics array. The fix moves the drain() check to immediately before each triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] write.

The fix moves the bounds check to immediately before each write to triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++].

After the loop, another triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] occurs. This allows writing past the end of the 64-element array.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.
commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
judge_reasoning: The finding identifies a real issue fixed by the commit: missing bounds validation of x (and y) before use. commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
judge_reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-29779) is about uninitialized entries in the 'deleted' array when Array.prototype.splice() is called on a non-fast-array 'this' that has gaps (missing properties). The fix adds an else branch that calls njs_set_invalid(&deleted->start[i]) to properly initialize those gap entries. This prevents a later segmentation fault in njs_value_own_enumerate when iterating the deleted array. The finding describes a completely different issue (heap buffer overflow via memcpy) that is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: When Array.prototype.splice() was called with a non-fast-array 'this' argument that had gaps (missing properties), and the 'deleted' result array was a fast array, the entries in deleted->start corresponding to the gaps were left uninitialized. Later access to these uninitialized entries (e.g., via njs_value_own_enumerate) caused a segmentation violation. The fix initializes gap entries in the deleted array with njs_set_invalid().

undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow write; attacker can overwrite adjacent heap objects, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.
commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
judge_reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37848) is specifically about timing side-channel information leakage because strncmp is used for hash comparison (CWE-200). The finding instead describes a null-byte truncation issue (CWE-187) where strncmp stops comparing at 0x00 bytes. While both problems stem from using strncmp on binary hash data and are fixed by the same patch, they describe fundamentally different attacks: the CVE is about timing oracle attacks to deduce hash contents, while the finding is about authentication bypass via null-byte truncation. The finding does not identify the timing side-channel vulnerability that is the actual CVE.
judge_actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a timing side-channel in password hash comparison. strncmp was used to compare password hashes, which leaks timing information because it returns early on the first differing byte. An attacker could observe response times to deduce hash bytes one at a time. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq, which performs constant-time comparison.

undesired_operation.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash digests. strncmp stops at the first null byte (0x00), so if either the computed or stored hash contains a null byte early, the comparison only checks bytes before that null byte. If the stored hash's first byte is 0x00, any password whose hash also starts with 0x00 will match (probability ~1/256 instead of cryptographic strength). If both hashes start with 0x00, strncmp returns 0 immediately and any password is accepted.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker can brute-force a password that matches only the bytes before the first null in the stored hash, dramatically reducing the search space. In the worst case (stored hash starts with 0x00), nearly any password is accepted.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
judge_reasoning: The actual CVE-2020-12723 is about mutating the regexp program within GOSUB recursive/enframed calls in S_study_chunk. The fix adds a `mutate_ok` boolean and `in_gosub` tracking to prevent inner recursive calls from modifying regops that an outer call is still examining. This finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in arithmetic expressions, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

The actual CVE-2020-12723 is about S_study_chunk mutating the regexp program during GOSUB recursion/enframing, where an inner call modifies nodes that an outer call is still processing. The fix introduces `mutate_ok` and `in_gosub` flags to prevent this. This finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow issue unrelated to the actual vulnerability.
judge_actual_issue_summary: S_study_chunk recursively processes GOSUB nodes by creating scan frames. During this recursive/enframed processing, inner calls could mutate the regexp program (e.g., JOIN_EXACT, TRIE optimization, CURLYX=>CURLYN/CURLYM conversions, EXACTFU_S_EDGE=>EXACTFU) while an outer call was still examining those same nodes. The fix adds a `was_mutate_ok`/`mutate_ok` parameter and an `in_gosub` flag on scan frames to prevent any mutation of regops when inside GOSUB handling, letting only the outermost level perform mutations.

undesired_operation.description: Signed integer overflow in the expression '(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount' when computing delta for CURLY quantifiers. This is undefined behavior in C and can result in delta becoming an incorrect (e.g., negative or wrapped) value, potentially causing downstream incorrect buffer size calculations.
undesired_operation.impact: Signed integer overflow leads to undefined behavior. In practice, the corrupted delta value propagates to buffer size calculations (e.g., RExC_maxlen), potentially leading to heap buffer overflow or denial of service.

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

The fix removes the spurious `+ 4` from both the output length calculation and the needed_data_count field, preventing 4 extra bytes from being sent back to the client.

When max_out_len >= 64, *out_len is set to 64, causing the caller to return 64 bytes to the client, leaking 16 bytes of uninitialized heap memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — 16 bytes of uninitialized heap memory (bytes 48-63) are returned to the client, potentially leaking sensitive data such as pointers, credentials, or other heap contents.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.
commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
judge_reasoning: The finding exactly matches CVE-2022-29192. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks that input_min and input_max are scalars when axis==-1, before calling .scalar<T>(). The finding correctly identifies that a 1-D tensor with size != 1 bypasses existing validation and triggers the CHECK failure.
judge_actual_issue_summary: When axis_ == -1, the code calls input_min_tensor.scalar<T>() and input_max_tensor.scalar<T>() without first verifying these tensors are actually scalars. A 1-D tensor with dims==1 but dim_size(0) != 1 passes the existing validation (which only checks dims, not num_elements) and the dim_size check is skipped when axis_==-1. This causes a CHECK failure crash (denial of service). The fix adds IsScalar checks before the scalar<T>() calls.

An attacker can supply a 1-D min/max tensor with dim_size(0) != 1 (e.g., 0 or >1 elements), bypassing the dims check (which allows dims==1) and the dim_size check (which is gated on axis_ != -1). The scalar<T>() accessor triggers a CHECK failure, crashing the process.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via CHECK-failure crash. An attacker providing crafted inputs to the TensorFlow op can crash the serving process.

The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. judge_reasoning: This finding directly describes the CVE-2022-23558 vulnerability: TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes returns an int, causing integer overflow when size is large. The overflowed value is stored in an int alloc_size, leading to an undersized allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow. The fix changes alloc_size to size_t to preserve the full computed size.
judge_actual_issue_summary: TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes returns an int, and the result is stored in an int variable `alloc_size` in TfLiteIntArrayCreate. When the input `size` is large, the computed allocation size overflows the int type, resulting in a small positive value that passes the <= 0 check. This leads to an undersized malloc allocation. The fix changes `alloc_size` from `int` to `size_t`.

When `size` is a large attacker-controlled int, the intermediate size_t result of the multiplication/addition wraps when truncated back to the `int` return type (`computed_size`), producing a small positive value. This undersized value is returned to the caller and typically used for memory allocation, leading to a heap buffer overflow when the caller populates the array.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — the function returns a small allocation size for a logically large array, leading to out-of-bounds writes when the caller fills the array. This can lead to arbitrary code execution.

judge_findings.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-24763) is an infinite loop in the CDATA content scanning section. When parsing CDATA, if the scanner encounters a ']' that is not followed by ']>', the while loop calls pj_scan_get_until_ch(scanner, ']', &dummy) without first advancing past the current ']', causing the scanner to never make progress. The fix adds pj_scan_advance_n(scanner, 1, PJ_FALSE) before that call. Finding 0 describes a different hypothetical infinite loop in the attribute parsing section, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.
